title: Bottom falls out of electricity demand in VIC on Christmas Day 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bottom falls out of electricity demand in VIC on Christmas Day 2020" posted at 28-12-2020 11:42:00 AM is: On Christmas Day 2020, electricity demand in Victoria (VIC) experienced a significant drop, with NEMwatch v10 showing a low demand of 2,259MW in the 12:50 dispatch interval and AEMO data indicating an Operational Demand as low as 2,529MW by the half-hour ending 13:00. This reduction in demand was attributed to the strong performance of rooftop PV, which led to a decrease in load from VIC brown coal units. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (during Christmas Day 2020). Its root_cause is: High levels of rooftop PV generation and low underlying demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-12-2020 (Christmas Day 2020). Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC). Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants in VIC. Its cause type is: Market-driven. Its causes are: Strong rooftop PV generation, reduced brown coal unit load, low operational demand.

title: Temperature Alert!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Temperature Alert!" posted at 24-12-2020 03:56:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges and developments in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the summer of 2020-21, highlighting the impacts of extreme temperatures and the La Nina weather pattern on both electricity consumption and supply. The piece covers how high temperatures increase electricity demand due to cooling needs but also lead to operational challenges for grid management. It also details new measures by AEMO to provide more localized temperature alerts, aiming to improve generation plant readiness and grid stability under high temperatures. Additionally, it highlights the importance of preparing for summer conditions, referencing recent educational efforts and resources by AEMO and industry bodies. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Summer 2020-21. Its root cause is: Extreme weather conditions and temperature fluctuations impacting electricity consumption and grid operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24th December 2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly South Australia, Victoria, and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Power generators and grid operators. Its cause type is: Natural and operational. Its causes are: High temperatures causing increased electricity demand, grid stress, and local temperature effects on generation assets leading to operational challenges.

title: How aggregate production from Wind and Large Solar in NSW dropped last week – one factor contributing to price spikes and Reserve Trader
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How aggregate production from Wind and Large Solar in NSW dropped last week – one factor contributing to price spikes and Reserve Trader" posted at 21-12-2020 09:45:00 AM is: The article discusses the price volatility experienced in the New South Wales (NSW) region of the National Electricity Market during the week of December 14, 2020. The volatility was attributed to multiple factors, notably the significantly reduced production from wind and large solar plants in NSW on several days compared to previous levels, particularly from December 15 to December 17. A critical event was on Thursday, December 17, when a transformer issue at Liddell unit 3 caused a long-term outage and the Reserve Trader was triggered due to system stress. Other contributing factors include transmission constraints within NSW, demand fluctuations, and constraints on interconnections to other regions. Despite only moderate levels of demand, the discrepancies between forecast and actual conditions led to significant price spikes at various times throughout the week. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (during the week of the event). Its root_cause is: Reduced wind and solar output, transformer failure at Liddell unit 3. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Monday 14th December 2020 to Friday 18th December 2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NSW region. Its cause type is: Technical/Operational. Its causes are: Transformer failure, reduced renewable energy output, transmission and interconnector constraints, unexpected demand levels.

title: What’s up with the fleet of 16 x coal-fired units in NSW?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s up with the fleet of 16 x coal-fired units in NSW?" posted at 21-12-2020 09:08:00 AM is: The article discusses the price volatility experienced in the New South Wales region of the National Electricity Market during mid-December 2020. Contributing factors include a transformer failure at Liddell unit 3, reduced performance of wind and solar production, transmission constraints within NSW and between NSW and Queensland, and unexpected demand spikes. Liddell, Bayswater, and other NSW coal units faced outages, with some being planned but unexpectedly extended, while others were forced. These events, combined with other systemic factors, led to significant price excursions in the electricity market. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, especially concentrated in mid-December 2020. Its root_cause is: Transformer failure and reduced availability of coal-fired units. Its accident scale is: Regional impact. Its dates are: 14th December - 18th December 2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW, including residential, commercial, and industrial sectors. Its cause type is: Technical and operational constraints. Its causes are: Transformer issue at Liddell unit, lower wind and solar output, transmission constraints, and unexpected demand spikes.

title: Liddell unit 3 out till perhaps March 2021 after transformer incident (serious injury to one person)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Liddell unit 3 out till perhaps March 2021 after transformer incident (serious injury to one person)" posted at 18-12-2020 11:27:00 AM is: A transformer incident at AGL's Liddell Power Station resulted in a significant outage of Unit 3, leading to its unavailability potentially until March 2021. This occurrence has also caused serious injury to one individual, who is receiving medical attention. The event has contributed to price volatility in the NSW electricity market and triggered the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) mechanism, highlighting the impact on power supplies during the summer period. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Up to two and a half months, until March 2021. The root cause is: Transformer incident. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-12-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Transformer failure at Liddell Power Station's Unit 3, leading to a trip and subsequent outage.

title: Increasing the transparency and correctness of intermittent generation availability information to AEMO
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Increasing the transparency and correctness of intermittent generation availability information to AEMO" posted at 18-12-2020 09:32:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges and importance of improving the transparency and accuracy of intermittent generation availability information in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the need for reliable and consistent plant availability data from wind and solar farm operators to enhance forecasting processes and maintain power system security. Transparency and traceability in data submission are essential for market efficiency and planning. The article emphasizes the necessity for cooperation between AEMO and market participants and suggests improvements such as more useable systems and better communication surrounding complex situations like extreme weather and distribution network limits.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with long-term implications on market efficiency and planning. Its root_cause is: Inadequate and inconsistent data submission from market participants affecting forecasting accuracy. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-12-2020. Its affected region is: Regions within the National Electricity Market, especially those depending on intermittent generation. Its affected key users are: Wind and solar farm operators, AEMO, and potentially electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Operational and data management issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Inaccurate plant availability information submission, extreme weather conditions, distribution network limits, and complexities in handling inverter-limit constraints.

title: Price volatility (third day in a row) … and Reserve Trader dispatched in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility (third day in a row) … and Reserve Trader dispatched in NSW" posted at 17-12-2020 03:32:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant period of price volatility in the New South Wales (NSW) electricity market, highlighting a dispatch price spike on December 17, 2020. AEMO responded by dispatching the Reserve Trader services due to an LOR1-level Low Reserve Condition warning, attributed to several factors, including multiple offline coal units due to delayed maintenance and reduced output from wind and solar sources. Additionally, constraints affected electricity flow between VIC and NSW due to transmission line issues.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact. Its root_cause is: Offline coal units, reduced renewable generation, and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-12-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Multiple operational issues. Its causes are: Offline coal units due to maintenance, lower wind and solar output, and transmission line constraints between VIC and NSW.

title: Wind and solar transactions: What to buy in the new-year sales
author: Ben Willacy
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind and solar transactions: What to buy in the new-year sales" posted on 16-12-2020 03:17:00 PM is: The article explores the current landscape of investment opportunities in Australia’s renewable energy market, particularly focusing on wind and solar energy assets. It identifies a substantial amount of capacity, approximately 5 GW, up for sale publicly, with a potential for an additional 8.5 GW below the surface. Driven by low capital costs and institutional capacity, long-term ownership consolidation may emerge as investors debate the NEM's market risk. Wind assets are favored over solar due to higher dispatch weighted prices and less need for firming. Tilt Renewables and Clarke Creek wind farms are highlighted as promising opportunities. Various market factors, including construction delays and suppressed daytime prices, dictate the urgent need for strategic investment selection. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Market dynamics and investment strategies in the renewable energy sector. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-12-2020 03:17:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Investors and renewable energy developers. Its cause type is: Economic and strategic. Its causes are: Increased capacity availability in the renewable energy market, strategic sales by asset owners, and market-driven investment consolidation in wind and solar sectors.

title: More price volatility in (QLD and) NSW on Wednesday 16th December 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More price volatility in (QLD and) NSW on Wednesday 16th December 2020" posted at 16-12-2020 02:46:00 PM is: The article discusses a series of electricity price spikes in the New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on December 15th and 16th, 2020. Factors contributing to the price volatility include ongoing maintenance of coal units delayed by COVID restrictions, a trip of the Eraring unit 3, and reduced output from the TUMUT3 unit. Additionally, the limited performance of wind and solar farms, some still in early commissioning stages, and a constraint on the QNI interconnector further exacerbated price increases. Notably, these constraints led to exceptionally high trading prices, such as $4,332.76/MWh for the half-hour ending 13:30, and $1,889.89/MWh for the half-hour ending 14:00 on December 16th, 2020. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root cause is: Limited coal-fired unit availability, interconnector constraints, and reduced renewable performance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-12-2020 and 16-12-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and market participants in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Mixed (including operational constraints, technical failures, and underperformance of renewables). Its causes are: Coal unit maintenance delays, Eraring unit 3 trip, TUMUT3 constrained output, QNI interconnector constraint, reduced wind and solar output.

title: Short price spike in NSW on Tuesday 15th December 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Short price spike in NSW on Tuesday 15th December 2020" posted at 15-12-2020 03:40:00 PM is: The occurrence of a price spike in the New South Wales electricity market on 15th December 2020 was primarily influenced by a series of events, including the trip of Eraring Unit 3 at a low load of 285MW to 0MW, unexpected maintenance catch-ups on coal units, and constraint issues affecting TUMUT3 due to a network outage, leading to the temporary spike in dispatch prices reaching $3,120.26/MWh at 12:50 PM. These conditions, though part of a complex interconnected system, underscore the challenges in electricity supply management and grid stability during the summer of 2020-2021. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (on the date of 15th December 2020). Its root_cause is: The trip of Eraring Unit 3 and subsequent network constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-12-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure and network constraint issues. Its causes are: Eraring Unit 3 trip, delayed maintenance on coal units, network outage leading to constraints on TUMUT3.

title: PPAs aren’t as easy as they used to be!
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "PPAs aren’t as easy as they used to be!" posted at 10-12-2020 09:43:00 AM is: The article discusses a series of webinars presented by the Clean Energy Council, highlighting key points related to semi-scheduled generators and the increasing complexity of Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs). The discussions focus on maximizing profitability in the National Electricity Market (NEM) for solar and wind farms, understanding generator obligations, optimizing operations, and adapting to market and power system changes. A specific mention is made of an ongoing rule change by the Australian Energy Regulator that was at the Draft Determination stage with the Australian Energy Market Commission in November 2020. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Increasing complexity of Power Purchase Agreements and rule changes in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17th September 2020, 10th September 2020, 18th May 2020, 19th November 2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled Generators, Solar and Wind Farms. Its cause type is: Policy/Regulatory changes. Its causes are: Complexity of PPAs, generator obligations, market adaptations, rule changes by AER and AEMC.

title: How does a wind or solar farm make money?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "How does a wind or solar farm make money?" posted at 09-12-2020 08:25:00 AM is: The article discusses a series of webinars presented by Jonathon Dyson and the author on maximizing value and profitability in the National Electricity Market (NEM) for solar and wind farms. Key points include understanding obligations as a Semi-Scheduled Generator, the importance of compliance over conformance, and strategies to maximize financial returns amid market and system changes. The webinars have also highlighted the distribution of spot prices and comparative performance of wind farms. Upcoming Clean Energy Council events in 2021 for both solar and wind sectors were also mentioned.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-12-2020 08:25:00 AM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes are: Webinars on maximizing value for solar and wind farms in the NEM, discussion on compliance versus conformance, market changes and strategies for financial optimization.

title: Constraint Case Study Followup – Early outcomes of the new “X5 constraint”
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Constraint Case Study Followup – Early outcomes of the new 'X5 constraint'" posted at 08-12-2020 11:11:00 AM is: The article discusses the impact of the N^^N_NIL_3 ('X5') constraint on the National Electricity Market, focusing on its binding behavior affecting dispatch outcomes, especially related to wind and solar generation. The constraint, which binds during daylight hours, significantly influences interregional electricity flows, particularly the VIC-NSW interconnector and Murraylink interconnector, resulting in economic impacts such as increased dispatch costs. The behavior underlines the complexities of NEM's region-wide spot pricing model and the resulting strategic bidding patterns by generators, known as "disorderly bidding." It also raises broader discussions on the potential benefits of a nodal pricing model for addressing inefficiencies. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The implementation and behavior of the N^^N_NIL_3 ('X5') constraint on the NEM grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-11-2020. Its affected region is: Victoria, New South Wales, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Generators, particularly those involved in interregional power transfers. Its cause type is: System operation. Its causes are: Binding constraints on electricity dispatch affecting interconnectors and regional power flows.

title: Conformance and compliance–what’s the difference?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Conformance and compliance–what’s the difference?" posted at 08-12-2020 08:14:00 AM is: The article highlights a series of webinars conducted by Jonathon Dyson and the author, focusing on 'Maximizing Value and Profitability in the National Electricity Market (NEM)' for solar and wind farms. The webinars discussed key actions that Semi-Scheduled Generators need to undertake to optimize returns while effectively adapting to market and power system changes. An emphasis was placed on understanding the distinctions between "Non-Conformance" and "Non-Compliance." Additionally, past webinars explored topics such as the changing distribution of spot prices and wind farm performance, while upcoming events are scheduled for 2021 by the Clean Energy Council. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Changing market conditions and power system adaptations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-12-2020, 17th September 2020, 18th May 2020, 10th September 2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Solar and Wind Farm operators. Its cause type is: Market adaptation requirements. Its causes are: The need to understand obligations and maximize returns in a changing electricity market environment.

title: Do you know your obligations as a Semi-Scheduled Generator?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Do you know your obligations as a Semi-Scheduled Generator?" posted at 07-12-2020 08:20:00 AM is: The article discusses a series of webinars conducted by Jonathon Dyson and the author on maximizing value and profitability for solar and wind farms in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The webinars, part of the Clean Energy Council's forum series, emphasize the compliance obligations for Semi-Scheduled Generators and are supported by articles released prior to each webinar, offering insights into spot prices and wind farm performance. The news also mentions the scheduling of the Clean Energy Council's Large-Scale Solar Forum and Wind Industry Forum for 2021 in Brisbane and Melbourne, respectively.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Informational content on compliance obligations for Semi-Scheduled Generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-12-2020 08:20:00 AM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled Generators in the NEM. Its cause type is: Informational. Its causes are: Webinars on compliance obligations, Insights on spot prices and farm performance, Scheduling future Clean Energy Council events.

title: Price spikes in NSW and QLD on the first afternoon of December
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spikes in NSW and QLD on the first afternoon of December" posted at 01-12-2020 05:31:00 PM is: On the afternoon of December 1st, a significant price spike occurred in New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) within the National Electricity Market (NEM), with prices reaching $3,867.31 in NSW and $3,001.66 in QLD during the 16:15 dispatch interval. This spike followed an unexpected drop in output of 343 MW at Liddell Power Station due to a unit trip during the 16:10 dispatch interval, causing short-term market volatility. Prices returned to typical levels soon after the event. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Unit trip at Liddell Power Station. Its accident scale is: Localized. Its dates are: 01-12-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational Incident. Its causes are: Sudden 343 MW output drop due to unit trip at Liddell Power Station.

title: Making demand response work with negative prices
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Making demand response work with negative prices" posted at 24-11-2020 06:31:00 AM is: The article discusses recent price volatility in the National Electricity Market (NEM) ahead of summer 2020, highlighting the need for effective demand response mechanisms amidst increasing incidents of negative electricity prices. The volatility was noted on several days in November in regions such as Queensland, New South Wales, and Tasmania. The news reflects on the challenges of incentivizing increased consumption during times of surplus solar and wind power generation, a necessity unaddressed by the recently introduced Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism set to commence in October 2021. Additionally, the article references efforts and mechanisms designed to manage consumption during periods of negative pricing to stabilize the grid, including Western Power’s initiative in WA. The piece underscores the importance of evolving demand response strategies to handle both high and negative pricing efficiently. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing, with potential long-term implications as demand response mechanisms evolve. Its root cause is: Surplus generation capacity from solar and wind, leading to negative electricity prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: November 15, 16, 17, 20, and 22, 2020. Its affected region is: NEM, particularly Queensland, New South Wales, Tasmania, and indirectly Western Australia for strategic response planning. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers, grid operators, and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market and grid operational challenges related to renewable energy integration. Its causes are: Increasing occurrence of negative electricity prices, inadequate demand response frameworks, and surplus renewable energy generation causing grid instability threats.

title: Surprise LOR2 (Low Reserve Condition) forecast for QLD next Monday afternoon, 30th November 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Surprise LOR2 (Low Reserve Condition) forecast for QLD next Monday afternoon, 30th November 2020" posted at 23-11-2020 08:12:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast of a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for Queensland on 30th November 2020, between 13:30 and 20:00. This follows an initial LOR1 warning and highlights potentially insufficient reserve capacity, as the minimum capacity available is expected to be significantly below the requirement. The forecast aims to prompt a market response to prevent the situation from materializing. Potential causes for this condition include the compressed outage season due to COVID-19 and possible data entry errors. The notice underscores the rarity of such warnings in the Queensland region, given the usual surplus in installed capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate - on 30th November 2020. Its root cause is: Investigating - possible compressed outage season and data entry errors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-11-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted grid reserve margin shortfall. Its causes are: Compressed outage season due to COVID-19 restrictions, potential 'fat finger error' in data entry.

title: Case Study – How to interpret a new NEM constraint and guess what it might do
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study – How to interpret a new NEM constraint and guess what it might do" posted at 23-11-2020 11:34:00 AM is: The article examines a new constraint named N^^N_NIL_3 in the National Electricity Market (NEM) affecting solar and wind generators, particularly in south-west New South Wales and north-west Victoria. This constraint limits power flow on a specific transmission line and can impact renewable generator dispatch if it becomes binding. The piece serves as a case study in interpreting NEM constraints, explaining how such constraints operate in guiding the NEM Dispatch Engine (NEMDE) to minimize costs while respecting the transmission network's secure levels. The constraint is currently non-binding but poses potential future effects as more solar farms become operational. By looking into the factors and mathematical formulation of the constraint, the analysis emphasizes the need to consider offer prices and transmission capacities when predicting NEMDE's decisions under binding conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Future potential as new solar farms near operational capacity. Its root cause is: Transmission limitations affecting renewable generators. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 23-11-2020. Its affected region is: South-west New South Wales and north-west Victoria. Its affected key users are: Renewable energy generators. Its cause type is: Technical. Its causes are: Transmission line constraints, increased renewable energy output, and potential power flow limitations on the X5 line between Balranald and Darlington Point.

title: More pain ahead for non-synchronous units … a bigger ‘rhombus of regret’ in western NSW & VIC
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More pain ahead for non-synchronous units … a bigger 'rhombus of regret' in western NSW & VIC" posted at 19-11-2020 07:34:00 PM is: The AEMO has issued a market notice regarding a new system constraint to prevent voltage collapse at Balranald in south-west NSW. This constraint involves the loss of a 220 kV line in north-west Victoria and includes generators in south-west NSW and north-west Victoria, affecting non-synchronous and semi-scheduled units predominantly in these areas. The constraint equation N^^N_NIL_3, labeled as the 'X5 constraint', is set to go into effect on 20th November 2020, creating potential binding conditions during high generation periods, particularly impacting solar and wind farms. This could lead to disorderly bidding and an increased push for nodal pricing due to the constraint's regional span. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: From 20th November 2020 until rectifications are made. Its root cause is: New system constraint to prevent voltage collapse. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-11-2020 and 20-11-2020. Its affected region is: South-west NSW and North-west Victoria. Its affected key users are: Non-synchronous and semi-scheduled units, specifically solar and wind farms. Its cause type is: Grid/system instability. Its causes are: Voltage collapse risk, high generation periods, and potential loss of transmission lines.

title: AEMC publishes draft rule change for Semi-Scheduled generators
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC publishes draft rule change for Semi-Scheduled generators" posted at 19-11-2020 05:16:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has released a draft rule change for Semi-Scheduled generators addressing challenges with their operations in the National Electricity Market (NEM). This includes ensuring that semi-scheduled generators meet dispatch targets unless variations due to resource availability occur. The draft rule aims to close loopholes that provide semi-scheduled generators with a competitive edge, making the market more technology-neutral. The changes are crucial due to the growing role of semi-scheduled generation, such as wind and solar, in the energy transition. Submissions on this draft rule are due by January 14, 2021, with final determinations expected on February 25, 2021.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Implementation of non-neutral rules on the operational methods of Semi-Scheduled generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-11-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled generators. Its cause type is: Regulatory and market reform. Its causes are: Challenges in operations of Semi-Scheduled generators, loopholes providing competitive advantages, the need for technology-neutral market rules.

title: An evolving perspective – forecast impacts of the South Australia short, sharp ‘Circuit Breaker’ COVID response
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An evolving perspective – forecast impacts of the South Australia short, sharp ‘Circuit Breaker’ COVID response" posted at 19-11-2020 03:42:00 PM is: The South Australian government's short-duration "Circuit Breaker" lockdown due to a fast-spreading COVID-19 strain has led to significant changes in electricity demand forecasts within the National Electricity Market (NEM). The stringent lockdown, forecasted to last until the morning of November 25, 2020, is expected to substantially decrease electricity demand from commercial users and possibly significant industrial consumers. Forecast tools show a notable drop in demand, particularly on November 23 and 24, 2020, possibly resulting in future minimum demand scenarios akin to conditions with minimal operational demand. This reduced demand scenario is further complicated by temperature fluctuations, which significantly affect electricity consumption in South Australia. A major concern is the interplay between lockdown-driven residential consumption and reduced commercial usage, which might exacerbate electricity system challenges if the lockdown persists into hotter weather. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (until November 25, 2020). Its root_cause is: COVID-19 induced lockdown response. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: November 19, 2020, to November 25, 2020. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Commercial and industrial energy users. Its cause type is: Forecast demand change due to lockdown. Its causes are: Stringent COVID-19 lockdown, reduced commercial electricity consumption, uncertainty in industrial demand, temperature-dependent consumption variations.

title: Price spikes in QLD region third day in a row (Tue 17th Nov 2020)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spikes in QLD region third day in a row (Tue 17th Nov 2020)" posted at 17-11-2020 03:41:00 PM is: The news discusses a series of electricity price spikes in Queensland, Australia, over several days, specifically highlighting a spike that occurred at 13:10 on November 17th, 2020. The article attributes the spike primarily to a constraint equation known as 'Q>>NIL_CLWU_RGLC,' which limited the output of several units across the region, with Gladstone coal-fired power station and Susan River and Childers Solar Farms being exceptions. The article suggests that market dynamics, such as unit constraints and possible reactive bidding, influenced the price spikes. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing until constraints are resolved. Its root_cause is: Constraint on the electrical grid due to system-normal operations to prevent overloads. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-11-2020 and previously mentioned dates such as 15-11-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational constraints in the grid system. Its causes are: Constraint equation 'Q>>NIL_CLWU_RGLC' limiting unit output, possibly reactive bidding behavior post-price spike.

title: NSW renewable energy plan seals death warrant for six gigawatts of coal
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW renewable energy plan seals death warrant for six gigawatts of coal" posted at 17-11-2020 09:45:00 AM is: The New South Wales (NSW) government's announcement of its renewable energy growth plans signals a significant shift away from coal, projecting that around 6 gigawatts of coal capacity will become redundant by 2030. This shift marks a challenging economic environment for current coal-based energy providers, such as AGL, Origin, and Energy Australia, urging them towards greater investment in renewable energy. The government plans to back the construction of 12 gigawatts of wind and solar capacity along with 2 gigawatts of storage. These changes are expected to decrease electricity prices by $15/MWh by 2030. The NSW strategy is set to alter the dynamics of coal and renewable energy markets within the region and is a response to the lack of federal direction on energy policy. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: By 2030. Its root_cause is: Implementation of a state renewable energy policy aimed at increasing renewables and decreasing coal reliance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-11-2020 09:45:00 AM. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Operators of coal-fired power stations and companies invested in coal energy in NSW. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: NSW government policy to increase renewable energy capacity, competitive pressures from renewables, and anticipated decline in coal-based energy profitability.

title: Early blast of summer drives NSW and QLD prices through the roof
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early blast of summer drives NSW and QLD prices through the roof" posted at 16-11-2020 02:42:00 PM is: The article discusses unexpected price spikes in electricity in New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) due to an early summer heatwave. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had issued warnings for low reserve conditions, but the sudden increase in temperature led to higher-than-forecasted demand and associated stress on the grid, resulting in significant volatility in energy prices and frequency control ancillary services (FCAS). Despite additional capacity becoming available, the demand far outpaced initial forecasts, leading to price spikes. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Early summer heatwave and associated increase in demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-11-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Weather-related demand surge. Its causes are: Early summer heatwave, increased electricity demand, low reserve conditions warning by AEMO, and limited forecast accuracy for wind and solar generation.

title: What’s “Primary Frequency Response” and why does it matter anyway?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s “Primary Frequency Response” and why does it matter anyway?" posted at 06-11-2020 09:00:00 AM is: The article discusses the significance of the Primary Frequency Response (PFR) in maintaining system frequency within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Frequency in AC power systems is crucial for balancing supply and demand. Poor frequency control can lead to system inefficiencies and vulnerabilities during disturbances. Historically, the NEM's Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) have aimed to manage frequency, but have not been effective for finer adjustments. The PFR is introduced as a mandatory requirement to ensure NEM generators automatically adjust outputs based on frequency deviations, thus improving overall frequency control. Initiated in October, its implementation demonstrates marked improvements in frequency stability. The regulation aims to mitigate frequency wandering caused by changes in supply and demand, contributing to system resilience. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing improvements in frequency control. Its root_cause is: The deteriorating frequency control performance within the NEM grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-11-2020 09:00:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) area, Australia. Its affected key users are: NEM operators and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Regulatory change implementation. Its causes are: Implementation of PFR for NEM generators, adoption of automatic frequency response mechanisms, and inadequacy of existing FCAS for maintaining tight frequency control.

title: Case Study Part 5 – Why did those constraint bind – in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Case Study Part 5 – Why did those constraints bind – in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020?" posted on 03-11-2020 06:34:00 PM is: The article provides an in-depth analysis of the electricity market constraints that occurred in Queensland on October 13, 2020, leading to a significant spike in the dispatch price to $15,000/MWh. The constraints on nine solar farms were due to the system strength requirements in northern Queensland, further exacerbated by a transmission outage. This event highlights the importance of system strength, referring to the grid's ability to withstand faults, and the necessary adjustments required as older synchronous plants exit the grid. The constraints led to an unexpected drop in solar generation, causing the market price spike. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) responded by adjusting the constraint equations to allow a gradual reduction in generation, preventing sudden spikes in the future.

Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term (a few weeks). Its root_cause is: System strength constraints due to transmission line outage and resultant changes in solar power dispatch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13th October 2020, with ongoing analysis up to 3rd November 2020. Its affected region is: Northern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Solar farms and electricity consumers facing high prices. Its cause type is: Technical grid constraints. Its causes are: Transmission line outage, low system strength, constraints on solar power dispatch.

title: Low point for demand in Victoria drops still lower on Sunday 1st November 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low point for demand in Victoria drops still lower on Sunday 1st November 2020" posted at 01-11-2020 01:54:00 PM is: The news highlights a record low in electricity demand in Victoria, setting a new all-time minimum for Scheduled Demand in the region on 1st November 2020, dropping below the previous record set on 6th September 2020. The low demand was captured in the NEMwatch v10 dashboard and the AEMO's measure of Operational Demand also marked a new low, falling below 3,000MW for the first time this month; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Residential satisfaction. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Short-term impact. Its root_cause is: Seasonal variation in demand. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates is: 01-11-2020. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Non-accidental. Its causes is: Seasonal demand changes during Spring, lower consumption trends.

title: AEMC proceeds to fast-track consideration of the AER’s semi-scheduled generator dispatch obligations rule change
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC proceeds to fast-track consideration of the AER’s semi-scheduled generator dispatch obligations rule change" posted at 28-10-2020 08:50:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has initiated a fast-track rule change process regarding the Australian Energy Regulator's (AER) semi-scheduled generator dispatch obligations, which entails moving directly to a draft determination senza formal public consultation, expected on 19 November 2020. This process is a response to operational challenges posed by semi-scheduled generators, such as automated curtailments during periods of negative pricing and broader supply-demand balancing concerns in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Stakeholders have been invited to offer feedback prior to the draft determination; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium term (beyond the immediate rule change publication to future operational impacts). Its root_cause is: Operational challenges in managing semi-scheduled generators and automated curtailments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-10-2020, with related developments mentioned on 30th September 2020, 15th October 2020, and 19th November 2020. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Regulatory/Operational. Its causes are: Challenges with semi-scheduled generators, automated negative pricing responses, and inadequate supply-demand balancing visibility.

title: Batteries vs. peaking plants – a case study from 13 October
author: Andrew Wilson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Batteries vs. peaking plants – a case study from 13 October" posted at 28-10-2020 02:25:00 PM is: This article analyzes a significant event in the Queensland electricity market on 13 October, where spot prices dramatically fluctuated, reaching the market price cap of $15,000/MWh before dropping to a market price floor of -$1,000/MWh. It highlights the performance of a University of Queensland battery, versus traditional peaking plants, during this volatility. The battery promptly responded to price spikes, capturing significant revenue, while peaking plants faced challenges such as start-up time and ramp rate constraints, limiting their ability to capitalize. The article suggests that as the energy market evolves, with more frequent constraint equations and the upcoming shift to 5-minute settlements, similar market events may become more common, presenting both challenges and opportunities for market participants. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Sudden dramatic fluctuation in electricity spot prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13 October 2020. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants, including generators and consumers. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and constraints. Its causes are: Sudden price spike, constraints on peaking plant responsiveness, limitations of current market settlement periods affecting asset profitability.

title: Brief note about NSW price spike on Friday 23rd October 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief note about NSW price spike on Friday 23rd October 2020" posted at 23-10-2020 04:45:00 PM is: The article discusses a series of electricity price spikes that occurred in the New South Wales region on the afternoon of 23rd October 2020. These spikes were forecast in advance by the P5 Predispatch forecast. The author briefly mentions ongoing analyses of other events, indicating the potential for further investigation into the triggers of these price spikes. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term impact on operational and trading activities on 23rd October 2020. Its root cause is: Market dynamics as indicated by forecast predispatch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-10-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity traders and market participants in the NSW region. Its cause type is: Market operation and forecasting issue. Its causes are: Advance forecast of price spikes, market dynamics during dispatch intervals.

title: AEMO releases ‘Operational management of low demand in South Australia’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases ‘Operational management of low demand in South Australia’" posted at 23-10-2020 02:25:00 PM is: The article discusses the release of AEMO's document titled 'Operational management of low demand in South Australia', which was released on 22nd October 2020. This document examines ongoing issues with low operational demand in South Australia. The reference to prior low demand events, such as the significant 300MW low point when South Australia relied almost entirely on solar energy, is highlighted. The discussion underscores complexities and challenges in managing energy at times of low demand and high solar generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing, as the issue relates to current and future management practices. Its root_cause is: High penetration of renewable energy, leading to periods of low operational demand and system balancing challenges. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-10-2020. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy grid operators and energy market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Operational and management challenges. Its causes are: High renewable energy penetration, low operational demand, and system balancing difficulties.

title: Case Study Part 4 – Who did What – in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study Part 4 – Who did What – in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020?" posted at 22-10-2020 07:11:00 PM is: The article elaborates on the unexpected electricity price spike in Queensland on October 13, 2020, which rose to $15,000/MWh due to the sudden curtailment of around 600MW of large solar generation across ten solar farms, mostly in northern QLD. The price spike was triggered by reduced grid demand in northern QLD influenced by rooftop PV, compounded by the constraint on the #821 transmission connection. The analysis examines the responses of various power plants during the spike, highlighting significant operational details of the plants in the Queensland grid. The complex interplay of these factors, as part of an evolving case study series, emphasizes the critical importance of location and system capabilities in energy events, offering deeper insights into market dynamics and systemic vulnerabilities. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but with potential insights for future systemic adjustments. Its root_cause is: Sudden solar generation curtailment due to grid demand reductions and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Regional within Queensland. Its dates are: 13-10-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Industrial energy users in the Ross Zone, among others. Its cause type is: Grid demand reduction and transmission constraint. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Reduction in grid demand due to rooftop PV, outage on the #821 transmission line, and sudden curtailment of large solar farms.

title: With increasing diversity, the low point of instantaneous aggregate Wind Generation across the NEM is nudging away from 0MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "With increasing diversity, the low point of instantaneous aggregate Wind Generation across the NEM is nudging away from 0MW," posted at 20-10-2020 07:19:00 PM is: The article discusses the increasing diversity in wind generation across the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting that while new capacity is being added, the variability in production remains unpredictable. The low points of wind generation have risen slightly from previous lows but still represent only a small percentage of installed capacity, emphasizing the growing importance of addressing extremes in energy production. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Variability in wind generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-10-2020. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users are: Renewable energy producers. Its cause type is: Operational variability. Its causes are: Increase in wind generation diversity; Unpredictable wind generation patterns; Continuation of low points despite capacity growth.

title: Questions prompted by that chart from the ‘Renewable Integration Study’
author: Patricia Boyce
classified_content: The summary of the news "Questions prompted by that chart from the ‘Renewable Integration Study’" posted at 20-10-2020 01:03:00 PM is: The article discusses various interpretations of a chart presented by AEMO's 'Renewable Integration Study', exploring future scenarios for renewable energy penetration in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The study models outcomes for wind and solar energy contribution in NEM by 2025 under different scenarios and highlights potential financial challenges for intermittent renewable energy generators. As renewable penetration increases, traditional pricing dynamics in the wholesale market are affected, potentially leading to reduced profitability for generators and changing the nature of investment incentives in the sector. These dynamics could deter necessary investment for meeting future energy demands, suggesting modifications in market design are required to ensure sufficient returns and the stability of the energy supply.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Transition to renewable energy and market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-10-2020 01:03:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Renewable energy investors and generators. Its cause type is: Economic and market design factors. Its causes are: Increased renewable energy penetration, reduced generator earnings, changing market dynamics.

title: Case Study Part 3 – digging still deeper, about what happened in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study Part 3 – digging still deeper, about what happened in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020" posted at 17-10-2020 06:50:00 PM is: This article is a detailed analysis of an unexpected spike in electricity prices in Queensland on October 13, 2020, where prices surged to $15,000/MWh. The primary cause identified is the binding of the constraint set 'Q-BCNE_821', triggered by a transmission outage, which led to a rapid reduction of solar farm outputs to 0MW, causing a price jump. The event was not forecasted by AEMO's predispatch data, highlighting a transparency issue as this information was private at the time. The article discusses the sequence and causes of the event, the lack of pre-warning in market data, and considers the potential for future analysis to better understand and predict such occurrences. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to 10:00 AM. Its root cause is: Constraint set Q-BCNE_821 binding due to a transmission outage. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 13-10-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar farm operators and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Grid management and system constraint. Its causes are: Binding of constraint set Q-BCNE_821, unintended ramp-down of solar farms, transmission outage from Bouldercombe to Nebo, unforecast grid demand drop triggering system strength issues.

title: ‘The Market’ means different timescales
author: Stephen Wilson
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘The Market’ means different timescales" posted at 16-10-2020 11:00:00 AM is: The article discusses the varying time scales involved in electricity market designs and operations. It highlights how different segments of the electricity market operate on different time horizons, from real-time operations to investment decisions spanning decades. Stephen Wilson emphasizes the complexity of market design, involving multiple models to predict outcomes and the interactions between electricity, gas, and environmental sustainability. The article also explores the structure of the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Eastern Australia and the role of price signals for investment in the context of market design. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Discussion on market design complexities and the need for different models for varying time scales. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates is: 16-10-2020 11:00:00 AM. The affected region is: Primarily Eastern Australia. The affected key users are: System operators, market operators, power plant operators, plant owners, and investors. The cause type is: Structural and operational complexities in the electricity market. The causes is: Varying timescales in market design, the need for multiple models, investor interest in sustainability, and market operations challenges.

title: Does the NEM need to be redesigned?
author: Stephen Wilson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Does the NEM need to be redesigned?" posted at 16-10-2020 10:48:00 AM is: The article discusses the potential need for redesigning Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) to accommodate increasing shares of variable renewable energy generation, which presents challenges due to their zero short-run marginal costs. A panel at the "ESIG Down Under" conference revealed differing opinions on whether major reforms or minor adjustments are needed. With growing attention on how renewable energy interacts within the grid, there are calls for a market design that balances these dynamics while maintaining efficiency and encouraging investment. The piece highlights the complexity of electricity markets, such as spot markets and involvement of various stakeholders across different timescales, and addresses concerns regarding system reliability and sustainability. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources with zero short-run marginal costs into the electricity market, prompting discussions about market design. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-10-2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Market operators, system operators, investors, and policy-makers in the electricity sector. Its cause type is: Debate on market design and policy. Its causes are: Increasing renewable energy deployment, zero short-run marginal costs, need for efficient market design to balance sustainability, reliability, and investment incentives.

title: Case Study Part 2 – did Rooftop PV kill Large Solar Farm output (and cause the price spike) in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study Part 2 – did Rooftop PV kill Large Solar Farm output (and cause the price spike) in QLD on Tuesday 13th October 2020?" posted at 14-10-2020 12:52:00 PM is: On October 13, 2020, Northern Queensland experienced a sudden drop in solar farm output by 600 MW due to system strength constraints triggered by lower aggregate consumption, which plummeted below critical alert levels partly due to rooftop PV generation. The resultant scarcity in supply escalated electricity prices up to $15,000/MWh during the 09:45 dispatch interval. An ongoing constraint set, 'Q-BCNE_821', in place since October 6, contributed to the situation by enforcing stronger constraint equations after a line outage, possibly leading to recurring issues until October 16. Industrial demand response mechanisms exacerbated the issue by reducing demand further, preventing the reactivation of solar farms. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: October 13-16, 2020. Its root_cause is: System strength constraints and a significant mismatch between supply and demand due to reduced consumption and constraints on solar farm operations. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: October 13, 2020. Its affected region is: Northern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Solar farms and large industrial consumers in the 'Ross' zone. Its cause type is: Technical and operational. Its causes are: The activation of Q-BCNE_821 constraints, reduced aggregate demand due to rooftop PV and potential consumption drop at mine sites, system strength constraint impacts, delayed data processing, and industrial load curtailment in response to high spot prices.

title: The wholesale demand response train is approaching the platform
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "The wholesale demand response train is approaching the platform" posted at 13-10-2020 01:09:00 PM is: The article discusses the impending implementation of the ‘Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism’ in the National Electricity Market (NEM) set to commence before summer 2021-22. The mechanism aims to enable large commercial and industrial consumers to actively participate in demand response, turning energy usage fluctuations into a potential revenue source. It highlights both opportunities and challenges for potential participants, such as the requirement for fast-acting load adjustments and the need for technological upgrades to meet dispatch targets. Significant emphasis is placed on flexible energy use and appropriate incentives to engage these users. Additionally, the article underlines the requirement for demand response service providers to register with AEMO, and the proposed solution of incorporating a Fast Start Inflexibility Profile to enhance the mechanism's functionality. The potential evolution towards a two-sided market is also noted. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Summer 2021-22 onwards. Its root cause is: Implementation of the Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its date is: 13-10-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions. Its affected key users are: Large commercial and industrial consumers. Its cause type is: Policy and mechanism implementation. Its causes are: Introduction of the Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism, technological and policy developments, energy market flexibility requirements.

title: Case Study part 1 – Queensland price spikes to $15,000/MWh at 09:45 on Tuesday 13th October 2020 … coincident with many Large Solar Farms (tripping**)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study part 1 – Queensland price spikes to $15,000/MWh at 09:45 on Tuesday 13th October 2020…" posted at 13-10-2020 11:55:00 AM is: The article discusses a significant price spike in the Queensland electricity market, where prices reached the Market Price Cap of $15,000/MWh on the morning of October 13th, 2020. This spike occurred coincidentally with a substantial drop in output from large solar farms in the region, which collectively reduced by 599MW between the 09:45 and 09:50 dispatch intervals. The cause of the spike was determined by various factors, including initial market conditions, expected demand, and generator bids. Despite the drop in solar output, the spike was set prior to the solar farms tripping, indicating other contributing factors. Additional analysis is anticipated to better understand the events and sequence that led to this price spike.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown; Its root_cause is: Coincident reduction in output from numerous QLD solar farms; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: 13-10-2020; Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia; Its affected key users is: Large energy users; Its cause type is: Coincident tripping of solar farms; Its causes is: Price spike, solar farm output drop, market conditions, generator bids.

title: Some are waking up to the shortcomings of current methods of support for ‘Anytime/Anywhere Energy’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some are waking up to the shortcomings of current methods of support for ‘Anytime/Anywhere Energy’" posted at 11-10-2020 06:49:00 PM is: The article discusses the inadequacies of current support methods for 'Anytime/Anywhere Energy' as outlined in the Generator Report Card 2018. It highlights the 'schism' caused by these methods, which impacts energy transition negatively due to a lack of focus on the time and location of energy generation. The article cites Google's and PowerLedger's recognition of the issues as steps towards addressing these challenges. However, it emphasizes that there are still unaddressed complexities around appropriate energy supply location and time, impacting new market entrants. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Inadequate support framework for 'Anytime/Anywhere Energy'. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-10-2020. Its affected region is: Potentially global with specific reference to the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: New entrants in the energy market. Its cause type is: Policy and structural inadequacies. Its causes are: Lack of focus on time and place in energy generation and market support methods.

title: The decline to zero continues… SA demand and whole-of-NEM both drop even lower on Sunday 11th October 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The decline to zero continues… SA demand and whole-of-NEM both drop even lower on Sunday 11th October 2020" posted at 11-10-2020 01:26:00 PM is: The article highlights a new record low in electricity demand across the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly in South Australia due to an increase in rooftop photovoltaic systems. The Scheduled Demand in South Australia dropped to 278 MW, overtaking the previous record set in September 2020. Similarly, Victoria, New South Wales, and Queensland experienced near-record lows in electricity demand, contributing to a new low for the whole of NEM at 14,120 MW. This reduced demand is linked to low wind output, despite market prices remaining positive. The concerns over the challenges posed by rising rooftop solar installations and their impact on grid stability are emphasized. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Increased rooftop PV installations leading to reduced demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-10-2020. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales, Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers/providers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market/System dynamics. Its causes are: Record low electricity demand, increased rooftop PV contributions, low wind output.

title: Which state pays for interconnectors?
author: Ben Skinner
classified_content: The summary of the news "Which state pays for interconnectors?" posted at 06-10-2020 01:07:00 PM is: The article discusses the contentious issue of funding interconnectors within the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. It highlights the historical context of state-based energy systems and the current challenges in dividing costs among states. With a $23 billion investment in the Integrated System Plan (ISP) over 20 years, the Energy Security Board faces the complex task of allocating interconnector funding fairly. The article also examines various funding mechanisms, including bilateral deals and the "Inter-Regional Transmission Use of System" charge, as well as the failed experiment of merchant transmission exemplified by the Basslink project. A proposed solution by TasNetworks involves sharing interconnector costs based on the benefits received by each state, though this approach brings its own complications. The idea of Commonwealth funding to ease disputes is also explored, albeit with potential fairness issues and moral hazards. The relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Funding allocation of interconnectors within the NEM. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 06-10-2020. The affected region is: Australia. The affected key users are: Unknown. The cause type is: Policy and economic mechanism. The causes are: Historical state-based energy systems, disputes over cost allocation, failure of merchant transmission projects, and complex economic modeling for cost-benefit analysis.

title: Incomplete thoughts – potential risks to the NEM in relation to COVID
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Impact of Coronavirus on the NEM from March 2020
classified_content: The summary of the news "Incomplete thoughts – potential risks to the NEM in relation to COVID" posted at 05-10-2020 09:01:00 PM is: The article discusses numerous risks that COVID-19 poses to the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Key concerns include physical risks to generation and transmission infrastructure, delayed maintenance due to COVID precautions, disruption in construction timelines of new wind and solar plants, and financial stress on retailers from reduced consumption and increased debt. The impact of these risks could be felt in the upcoming summer if demand spikes during lockdowns. Furthermore, financial risks are emphasized, particularly the challenges retailers may face if energy users are unable to pay their bills. Some smaller retailers, particularly those in the residential sector or without robust hedging strategies, could face significant stress. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Summer 2020-21. Root_cause: COVID-19 pandemic. Accident scale: Potentially significant impact on NEM infrastructure and market stability. Dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 05-10-2020 09:01:00 PM. Affected region: Victoria, with concerns extending to broader NEM regions including NSW and QLD; specific transmission risks mentioned for Victoria. Affected key users: Electricity retailers and generators, particularly coal-fired units; indirectly, commercial, industrial, and residential energy users. Cause type: Pandemic-related operational challenges and economic impacts. Causes (a summarized list of cause events): Deferred maintenance on power plants, disrupted construction of new renewable energy projects, increased complexity in outage management, economic downturn reducing energy consumption, financial strain on energy retailers, possible spikes in energy demand due to lockdowns.

title: Case Study of 16:55 on Monday 8th April 2019 (aggregate Raw Off-Target +318MW for Semi-Scheduled units)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of 16:55 on Monday 8th April 2019 (aggregate Raw Off-Target +318MW for Semi-Scheduled units)" posted at 04-10-2020 07:29:00 PM is: The article explores the occurrence of substantial deviations in energy production by Semi-Scheduled units in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly at 16:55 on April 8, 2019. This event is part of broader analysis highlighting a growing trend throughout 2019 where such units frequently underperformed, contributing to grid instability. The focus is to understand the implications of these deviations, and whether the current operational frameworks for integrating solar power are adequate as solar capacity increases. The study also touches on different performance levels for various power generation units, suggesting the need for adaptations to manage the intermittencies that come with renewable energy sources.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term and potentially ongoing with the increasing penetration of solar energy. Its root_cause is: The underperformance and overperformance of Semi-Scheduled units, primarily solar and wind projects, during specific dispatch intervals. Its accident scale is: An aggregate Raw Off-Target of +318MW. Its dates are: 04-08-2019 and analysis extended to 2019. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity market operators and stakeholders in the NEM, including consumers affected by potential price changes and reliability issues. Its cause type is: Operational mismatches between predicted and actual power output from renewable energy sources. Its causes are: A deviation in expected power output from solar and wind farms, possibly due to factors like ramp rates and unforeseen weather changes impacting solar and wind energy production.

title: AER releases proposed rule change for Semi-Scheduled generators and dispatch instructions
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER releases proposed rule change for Semi-Scheduled generators and dispatch instructions" posted at 30-09-2020 05:01:00 PM is: The article discusses a proposed rule change by the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) submitted to the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC), focusing on clarifying dispatch instructions for semi-scheduled generators within the National Electricity Rules (NER). It aims to ensure these generators follow available resources except when restricted by network conditions or specified output caps during semi-dispatch intervals. The proposal arises from concerns about semi-scheduled generators' deviations, as highlighted by frequent incidents of significant off-target generation since 2019. The rule change intends to retain flexibility while providing clear guidelines for generator operations.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Concerns about generation off-target performance by semi-scheduled generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-09-2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled generators. Its cause type is: Regulatory. Its causes are: Concerns raised by the COAG Energy Council and increasing incidents of off-target generation by semi-scheduled units.

title: Case Study of 17:05 on Friday 5th April 2019 (aggregate Raw Off-Target +346MW for Semi-Scheduled units)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of 17:05 on Friday 5th April 2019 (aggregate Raw Off-Target +346MW for Semi-Scheduled units)" posted at 29-09-2020 05:03:00 PM is: The article presents an analysis of a critical underperformance event observed on 5th April 2019 in the National Electricity Market (NEM), focusing on semi-scheduled units underperforming by a net aggregate of +346MW. It delves into specific instances such as the Bungala 1 Solar Farm and Lake Bonney 2 Wind Farm, which experienced significant raw off-target deviations, contributing to the overall imbalance. The study is part of a larger examination into the rise of extreme off-target events in the NEM and aims to highlight the challenges within the dispatch interval timeframe, pushing for insights and resolutions in the so-called 'NEM 2.0'. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specific to dispatch intervals on 5th April 2019. Its root_cause is: Underperformance and unpredictability of semi-scheduled units at specific intervals. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 5th April 2019. Its affected region is: South Australia, with specific reference to units like Bungala 1 Solar Farm and Lake Bonney 2 Wind Farm. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical and operational issues within semi-scheduled units. Its causes are: Large under-performance of multiple semi-scheduled units leading to significant raw off-target values; specific constraints affecting units like Bungala 1 Solar Farm and Lake Bonney 2 Wind Farm.

title: Rooftop PV drives daytime Scheduled Demand even lower than the low point a month ago … lowest level in 16 years on Sunday 27th September 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Rooftop PV drives daytime Scheduled Demand even lower than the low point a month ago … lowest level in 16 years on Sunday 27th September 2020" posted at 27-09-2020 02:15:00 PM is: On 27th September 2020, Scheduled Demand in Queensland's electricity market reached a record low of 3,712MW at 11:30 AM, breaking the previous month's low of 3,889MW set on 23rd August 2020. This decline is attributed to the increased use of rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems, which have reduced demand from the grid. Despite acknowledging the challenges posed by this trend, such as the cannibalization of essential services needed to maintain grid stability, these issues remain unresolved. The operational and market demands present distinct measurements of grid consumption and prices, complicating historical comparison.
