Its affected key users are: Residential consumers, SMEs, and large businesses. Its cause type is: Social/economic change due to pandemic restrictions. Its causes are: COVID-19 lockdowns, remote working, reduced business operations, bushfire destruction, and altered residential energy use.

title: The NEM consumes (at least) 10GW of supply from renewable plant on Sunday 19th July 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The NEM consumes (at least) 10GW of supply from renewable plant on Sunday 19th July 2020" posted at 19-07-2020 11:30:00 AM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia recorded a significant achievement by surpassing a 10GW aggregate supply from renewable sources on July 19, 2020. The peak renewable supply reached approximately 10,275MW at 11:40 AM, with significant contributions from wind and solar power, while hydroelectric power decreased as solar production increased. Renewable supply was primarily driven by wind and solar, with solar divided between rooftop photovoltaic systems (60%) and large solar installations (40%). Despite differing production estimates from APVI and AEMO, the integration of renewables caused electricity prices to fall to zero during several dispatch intervals, marking a potential shift towards increased reliance on renewables during less demanding periods like weekends. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on the day of the event. Its root_cause is: Increased renewable energy generation leading to excess supply and reduced electricity prices. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates is: 19-07-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, Australia. Its affected key users is: Energy suppliers and consumers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Renewable energy supply dynamics. Its causes is: High renewable energy generation, low demand, price decrease.

title: Four headline challenges amongst a very busy summer 2019-20
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2020 – 31st January – Islanding of South Australia after transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four headline challenges amongst a very busy summer 2019-20" posted at 18-07-2020 03:25:00 PM is: The summer of 2019-20 was marked by four critical events in the Australian electricity market, affecting supply and demand dynamics and highlighting vulnerabilities in the system. Extreme temperatures in December 2019 caused demand spikes and forecast wind output to decrease significantly. Bushfires in early January 2020 led to the separation of Victoria and New South Wales from the National Electricity Market (NEM). At the end of January, South Australia experienced islanding due to transmission tower destruction from a wind event, severely impacting grid stability. The ongoing challenges from these events emphasized the escalating risks associated with extreme weather and system limitations. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather conditions, including high temperatures, bushfires, and high winds. Its accident scale is: Significant, affecting multiple regions and the overall stability of the NEM. Its dates is: December 2019 to early February 2020. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, and New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Energy consumers, grid operators, and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Natural and system-based causes. Its causes is: Extreme temperatures, bushfires damaging transmission lines, high wind events causing infrastructure damage, and resulting grid instability.

title: Semi-scheduled generation–what are the real issues?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Semi-scheduled generation–what are the real issues?" posted at 17-07-2020 02:47:00 PM is: The AER published a consultation paper proposing amendments to rules governing semi-scheduled generation, which has sparked debate over the best approach to align these generators' output with dispatch targets like scheduled generators. Key issues identified include market regulatory concerns, where semi-scheduled generators reduce output below dispatch level for non-physical reasons, and technical problems of rapid generation adjustments impacting system frequency. Proposed solutions include making rules more stringent for semi-scheduled generation, but concerns arise over the potential financial burden and technical implications, such as lost revenue and reduced forecast accuracy. The paper suggests the NZ model as a potential approach to manage curtailment behavior while exploring technical adjustments for smoother operations of wind farms. The complexity of these problems necessitates careful regulatory and operational cooperation for NEM 2.0. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, as regulatory changes and market behaviors adjust. Its root_cause is: Market and technical issues related to the behavior of semi-scheduled generators and impact on grid frequency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same as the main date, 17-07-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy regulators, semi-scheduled generators. Its cause type is: Market behavior and technical constraints. Its causes are: Semi-scheduled generators curtailing output deliberately for market reasons, rapid changes in output disrupting frequency stability.

title: Difficult to determine (i.e. minor) reduction in daily NEM-wide aggregate ‘Underlying Consumption’ in 2020, the (first?) COVID year
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Impact of Coronavirus on the NEM from March 2020
classified_content: The summary of the news "Difficult to determine (i.e. minor) reduction in daily NEM-wide aggregate ‘Underlying Consumption’ in 2020, the (first?) COVID year" posted at 13-07-2020 01:02:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of COVID-19 on electricity consumption in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting that while there were initial concerns, the overall NEM-wide daily aggregate consumption experienced only a slight reduction during the first half of 2020. The underlying consumption trends suggest that the NEM has largely escaped significant impacts from COVID-19 restrictions, with only minor decreases noted. The analysis considers potential discrepancies caused by factors such as weather variations and different customer segments but does not delve into regional or time-specific effects. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: February to July 2020. Its root_cause is: COVID-19 pandemic. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same as the main date. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Social and behavioral changes due to COVID-19. Its causes are: Lockdown measures, shifts in consumption patterns due to stay-at-home directives.

title: AEMC issues 3-month delay to the commencement of Five Minute Settlement
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC issues 3-month delay to the commencement of Five Minute Settlement" posted at 09-07-2020 06:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) announced a three-month delay in the implementation of the Five Minute Settlement and Global Settlement due to disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. Originally scheduled for July 1, 2021, the new commencement date is set for October 1, 2021, allowing stakeholders more time to adapt to changes amidst pandemic challenges; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until October 2021. Its root_cause is: Disruption due to COVID-19 pandemic. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-07-2020. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: External disruption. Its causes are: COVID-19 pandemic impacts on the working environment and energy industry.

title: It’s good news that time-weighted average prices for Q2 2020 are down on prior years – right?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It’s good news that time-weighted average prices for Q2 2020 are down on prior years – right?" posted at 09-07-2020 01:27:00 PM is: The article discusses the significant decrease in average electricity prices in Q2 2020 across various regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM), following the severe summer conditions and COVID-19 impacts. While lowered prices are beneficial for some energy users, they pose challenges for stakeholders reliant on higher spot market prices. The analysis highlights historical pricing patterns, the increased occurrence of negative prices, and futures pricing discrepancies that might financially disadvantage some market participants. Ultimately, the changing price dynamics suggest a complex and volatile electricity market landscape. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term. Its root_cause is: Decreased demand due to COVID-19, lower gas prices, and increased occurrences of negative pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same as the main date, 09-07-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions including QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, TAS, and the WA SWIS. Its affected key users are: Energy market stakeholders and consumers. Its cause type is: Market trends and dynamics. Its causes are: COVID-19 impact on demand, decreased gas prices, and changes in energy generation mixes leading to price volatility and negative prices.

title: ‘Too Low for Zero’ as a parable for the AER Issues Paper?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Too Low for Zero’ as a parable for the AER Issues Paper?" posted at 09-07-2020 01:21:00 PM is: The article discusses the implications of the AER Issues Paper, using the metaphor of Elton John's song "Too Low for Zero" to illustrate the complexities within the National Electricity Market (NEM) rules. It highlights different stakeholder perspectives on generation obligations, especially in relation to negative pricing and Semi-Scheduled generators. The AER Issues Paper suggests that the current rules, particularly regarding Semi-Scheduled generation, may pose a risk to system security and require revision to ensure sustainable market operations. The author reflects on whether current deliberations for NEM 2.0 address the entire scope of the problem and if the resources devoted to these discussions are adequate in solving emerging issues.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Discussion on current NEM rules and their impact on market sustainability and system security. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 09-07-2020 01:21:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and market rules analysis. Its causes is: Negative pricing, differing interpretations of Semi-Scheduled generator obligations, AER Issues Paper findings.

title: ‘Energy Technology – performing under (heat) stress’ – discussion on Friday 17th July 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'Energy Technology – performing under (heat) stress' – discussion on Friday 17th July 2020" posted at 08-07-2020 08:22:00 PM is: The announcement details an upcoming webinar where the author, alongside Judith Landsberg from the Bureau of Meteorology, will discuss how different generation technologies cope with heat stress, particularly following the challenging summer of 2019-20. The event is scheduled for Friday, 17th July 2020, at noon. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term during the summer of 2019-20 and ongoing future interest. Its root_cause is: Energy technology performance under heat stress conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-07-2020 and 17-07-2020. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technological and weather-related. Its causes are: Summer heat effects on generation technology.

title: Investigating the action on a trading interval boundary using the latest ez2view v7.4.5.665
author: Marcelle Gannon
topic: How you might use our software
classified_content: The summary of the news "Investigating the action on a trading interval boundary using the latest ez2view v7.4.5.665" posted at 08-07-2020 12:41:00 PM is: This article examines the latest release of ez2view v7.4.5.665, focusing on its enhancements in providing real-time and historical dispatch data more accurately, which is critical for understanding trading interval actions, particularly in events of negative pricing driven by low demand and high solar generation in Queensland. The update adds clarity between initial and final dispatch quantities, specifically aiding users during analysis of temporal data changes. The piece also highlights a case study of different outcomes at two solar farms - SMCSF1 and LILYSF1 - during a specific dispatch interval on 4 July 2020, illustrating the impacts of bid variations, semi-dispatch caps, and interconnector flows on electricity generation and pricing. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term. Its root_cause is: Software and bid configuration updates. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Solar energy operators and electricity market analysts. Its cause type is: Operational and market-based. Its causes are: Negative price events driven by high solar output, user confusion regarding dispatch data interpretation, and enhancements in software for better clarity and market analysis.

title: Saturday 4th July 2020 sees QLD spot prices crunched over long periods of the day, with solar booming
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Saturday 4th July 2020 sees QLD spot prices crunched over long periods of the day, with solar booming" posted at 04-07-2020 04:38:00 PM is: On July 4, 2020, Queensland experienced significantly reduced electricity spot prices due to high solar energy generation and low scheduled demand. Factors contributing to low demand included the pleasant winter temperatures, reducing the need for heating or cooling, and substantial input from rooftop solar PV systems exceeding 1,000MW. With the decreased demand and increased solar generation, prices plummeted below $0/MWh during most daylight hours. Automated bidding processes at solar farms like Whitsunday and Susan River highlighted challenges in market operations, with issues in following dispatch instructions and negative pricing cycles. These dynamics reflect ongoing complexities in the National Electricity Market's energy transition. Its relevance level is: 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing. Its root_cause is: Excess solar generation combined with low demand. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 04-07-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Solar farms and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Market conditions. Its causes are: High solar generation, pleasant weather reducing thermal demand, and market pricing mechanisms leading to negative spot prices.

title: ‘NEM merchant pricing–is this the new normal?’ – discussion on Wednesday 15th July 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘NEM merchant pricing–is this the new normal?’ – discussion on Wednesday 15th July 2020" posted at 04-07-2020 02:38:00 PM is: Invitations were sent by Vestas for a virtual discussion on the evolution of the National Electricity Market (NEM), reflecting recent developments. This event, scheduled for July 15, 2020, aims to cover ongoing insights into NEM's dynamics, with some information to be shared via WattClarity and further insights to be included in the Generator Report Card.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Ongoing developments in NEM merchant pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2020, 15-07-2020. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market evolution and discussion. Its causes are: Merchant pricing dynamics in the NEM, recent developments in market conditions.

title: Trended daily aggregate wind output since January 2010
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trended daily aggregate wind output since January 2010" posted at 29-06-2020 02:59:00 PM is: The article discusses trends in aggregate wind production since 2010, noting that despite increased capacity, the growth in output has not mirrored this, due to factors like commissioning delays, real-world operational challenges, and penalties affecting wind farms. It highlights the widening gap between peak output and installed capacity, issues with negative pricing, and constraints impacting generation. Nonetheless, increased diversity in sources has reduced the frequency of rock-bottom output levels, though low output events still occur. Additionally, lulls in wind generation have been identified as occurring 24 hours apart.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term since observations span back to January 2010. Its root cause is: Operational challenges and market conditions impacting wind farm utilization. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 19th April 2020 and 11th June 2020 (notable low output days); main article date is the same. Its affected region is: Northern and Southern Australia (focusing on the National Electricity Market zones). Its affected key users are: Wind farm operators and energy market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Market and operational conditions. Its causes are: Commissioning delays, simplistic modeling vs. real-world complexities, negative pricing leading to curtailment, system strength constraints, and increased operational diversity.

title: AER releases Issues Paper on proposed rule change(s) for Semi-Scheduled generators
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER releases Issues Paper on proposed rule change(s) for Semi-Scheduled generators" posted at 28-06-2020 05:12:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has released an Issues Paper to initiate discussions on potential rule changes concerning the operation of Semi-Scheduled generators in the electricity dispatch process. This move stems from the recognition that the current process is not sustainable or scalable. Concerns highlighted include behaviors like those observed at the Lake Bonney 2 Wind Farm. A forum to discuss these issues is scheduled for 2nd July, with submissions due by 22nd July, potentially influencing future rule changes at the AEMC; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term (as it involves policy discussions and changes). Its root cause is: The need for a more sustainable and scalable system for semi-scheduled generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-06-2020, 24-06-2020, 2-07-2020, 22-07-2020. Its affected region is: Australia (implied by the AER and AEMC involvement). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy/Regulatory. Its causes are: Sustainability and scalability concerns, previous generator behavior, and statistical analyses highlighting issues.

title: Why controlling residential solar inverters is more complicated than it appears
author: Joseph Kassouf
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why controlling residential solar inverters is more complicated than it appears" posted at 22-06-2020 11:38:00 AM is: The article discusses the complexities involved in controlling residential solar inverters to enhance integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) such as solar panels and batteries into the grid. SwitchDin experiences challenges due to a lack of standardized communication protocols among inverter manufacturers and widespread variations in implementation. Control of inverters involves overcoming hurdles like proprietary protocols, write cycle limits on control registers, varying control capabilities, and the logistical challenges of updating firmware. The article emphasizes the importance of establishing coordination across the energy system to maximize the benefits of DERs, exploring both hardware and cloud API integrations. Initiatives like the ARENA-backed evolve project aim to implement coordination, addressing both network and market needs, which includes considerations for fairness and management of prosumer assets. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term as it relates to ongoing integration efforts. Its root_cause is: Complexity and lack of standardization in inverter communications and control protocols. Its accident scale is: Unknown as no specific accident is reported. Its dates are: 22-06-2020 (same as the main date). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential solar PV system owners. Its cause type is: Technical and policy-related. Its causes are: A lack of standard communication protocols, proprietary communication methods, firmware updating challenges, and diverse inverter capabilities.

title: DER Visibility and Monitoring Best Practice Guide – real time data is key to industry growth
author: Stefan Jarnason
classified_content: The summary of the news "DER Visibility and Monitoring Best Practice Guide – real time data is key to industry growth" posted at 22-06-2020 11:03:00 AM is: The energy industry is swiftly transitioning into a distributed electricity network primarily driven by increased consumer demand for Distributed Energy Resources (DER) like rooftop solar and the growing prevalence of renewables as the least expensive energy source. With a forecast that 45% of generation will be behind the meter by 2030 in Australia, the lack of DER visibility is hindering efficient grid integration. A new DER Visibility and Monitoring Best Practice Guide promises to address this by detailing the necessary data for increasing DER deployment while maintaining grid reliability and promoting renewable energy. Developed over a year by industry leaders, this voluntary guide aligns with various initiatives spearheaded by AEMO and lays out static and dynamic data requirements to ensure minimal data collection for maximum benefit. These developments are projected to facilitate equitable DER increases, enhance DER quality, and bolster consumer confidence. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Over the next decade, until 2030. Its root_cause is: Transformation to a distributed electricity network driven by DER growth and renewable adoption. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2020 11:03:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy industry stakeholders and energy consumers. Its cause type is: Structural transformation in the energy sector. Its causes are: Increased consumer demand for DERs like rooftop solar, the rise of renewables as lowest-cost generation options, limited real-time data visibility of existing DERs.

title: AEMO releases its ‘2019-20 NEM Summer Operations Review Report’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases its ‘2019-20 NEM Summer Operations Review Report’" posted at 22-06-2020 09:38:00 AM is: The AEMO has released its Summer Operations Review Report, shedding light on the challenging summer of 2019-20 in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM). This period was marked by extreme weather events, including bushfires, which led the AEMO to intervene 178 times, as well as high temperatures that impacted solar and wind energy outputs. These stressors pushed the grid to its limits, highlighting the critical issues that need to be addressed in the ongoing energy transition. The report emphasizes the necessity of learning from these events to mitigate future risks amidst broader societal impacts such as COVID-19.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2019-20. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather events, including bushfires and high temperatures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, Australia. Its affected key users are: AEMO and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Extreme weather, bushfires, high-temperature impacts on generation supply technology.

title: What’s been happening with Bald Hills Wind Farm
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s been happening with Bald Hills Wind Farm" posted at 12-06-2020 08:00:00 PM is: The article discusses two significant changes in the Bald Hills Wind Farm's output over an 18-month period, with a sharp decline in performance starting in late September 2019 and notably reduced output from early June 2020, where output barely exceeded 0MW. While the registered participant, Alinta Energy Retail Sales Pty Ltd, has been bidding the plant at the market price floor (-$1,000/MWh), the cause of the reduced output remains unclear, with possibilities including physical plant limitations or environmental conditions. Observations regarding bid behavior and operation of the Automatic Wind Energy Forecasting System (AWEFS) were discussed, but no definitive conclusion regarding the low output was reached. The output began to recover on June 13th. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 18 months. Root_cause is: Unknown, potentially physical plant limitations or environmental conditions. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: September 2019 - June 2020, with a notable change on 31st May 2020. Affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Affected key users are: Unknown. Cause type is: Technical/Environmental. Causes are: Reduction in output potentially due to plant limitations or environmental conditions, unexplained bidding behavior, and the workings of AWEFS not fully transparent.

title: The rise of the ‘Negawatt Factory’?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The rise of the ‘Negawatt Factory’?" posted at 10-06-2020 10:08:00 PM is: The article discusses the anticipated final rule determination from the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) regarding the 'Negawatt Dispatch Mechanism,' which is expected on 11th June. This mechanism aims to integrate demand response into the energy market by promoting 'Negawatt Factories.' The author examines potential issues with the proposed rule changes, expressing concerns about the possibility of market manipulation due to intangible and unmeasurable new commodities like 'ghost electrons' and 'imaginary Negawatts.' The discussion also highlights possible pitfalls in the design of incentive structures and the potential mismatch between intent and outcomes. The author stresses the importance of a clear transition path to a properly functioning two-sided market as part of future energy reforms.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The new mechanism is set to commence on 1st October 2021. Its root_cause is: Introduction of the Negawatt Dispatch Mechanism by the AEMC. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: New rule determination expected on 11th June 2020; the mechanism to commence on 1st October 2021. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy users engaged in demand response programs. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: Establishment of a new demand response mechanism, introduction of intangible commodities, potential for market manipulation, and the need for a transition to a two-sided market.

title: ‘Dunkelflaute’ Down Under
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'Dunkelflaute' Down Under" posted at 10-06-2020 04:18:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant energy market event that occurred on the evening of June 9, 2020, in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Due to a significant drop in output from intermittent renewable sources like wind and solar, along with a high demand of 30,000 MW, electricity prices spiked across the mainland NEM regions. This situation, termed 'dunkelflaute' or 'a dark lull,' underscores the challenges of relying heavily on renewables during times of low renewable energy generation. The article highlights the need for strategic demand response and improved grid reliability as crucial elements for future energy stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term (9-11 June 2020). Its root cause is: Low renewable energy output and high demand. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 9 June 2020. Its affected region is: Mainland regions of the NEM in Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Natural/climatic conditions. Its causes are: Low wind and solar output, high electricity demand, inadequate supply-demand balance.

title: Why Have Home Battery Forecasts Been Staggeringly Wrong For Years?
author: Ronald Brakels
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why Have Home Battery Forecasts Been Staggeringly Wrong For Years?" posted at 10-06-2020 02:51:00 PM is: The article examines why predictions about home battery uptake in Australia were overly optimistic, highlighting that financial analysts in 2016 expected far greater adoption by 2020. Despite high electricity prices and subsidies, only 73,000 home batteries were installed, significantly below forecasts. The main reason cited for the missed predictions is that home batteries, even with potential savings from Virtual Power Plants, don’t yet pay for themselves for the average household. Causes of prediction errors include techno-optimism, reactions to anti-renewable sentiment, and deliberate market strategies. Analysts overestimated market growth without considering that each price decrease did not significantly expand the market. The uptake of batteries will depend on them becoming a cost-effective solution, a development that has not yet been realized. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long term (beyond 2020 until batteries become cost-effective). Its root cause is: Financial analysts' overly optimistic predictions on market adoption rates and economic feasibility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: October 6, 2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Prediction error due to market misjudgment. Its causes are: Home batteries not currently paying for themselves, techno-optimism, market reaction to anti-renewables sentiment, and deliberate market strategy.

title: Looking gloomy at Oakey 2 Solar Farm again?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking gloomy at Oakey 2 Solar Farm again?" posted at 09-06-2020 07:15:00 PM is: The Oakey 2 Solar Farm has faced significant operational challenges due to extreme weather events, including storms in October 2018 and January 2020, causing substantial damage and delays in commissioning. These setbacks have limited the plant's output until recently and have also impacted the farm's valuation. The farm's operational history highlights the complexities of renewable energy infrastructure under extreme conditions and grid compliance requirements.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, given structural damage and commissioning delays. Its root_cause is: Storm damage during construction and subsequent grid compliance issues. Its accident scale is: Significant damage affecting approximately 15% of the site in January 2020. Its dates are: 25th October 2018; January 2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Renewable energy market stakeholders and investors. Its cause type is: Environmental and regulatory. Its causes are: High wind speeds causing "torsional galloping," storm-induced physical damage, and grid compliance issues delaying full operations.

title: Feedback we have received about the 2019 edition of our Generator Statistical Digest
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Feedback we have received about the 2019 edition of our Generator Statistical Digest" posted at 08-06-2020 10:51:00 AM is: The 2019 edition of the Generator Statistical Digest, released on January 28, 2020, provided an update and extension to the previous Generator Report Card 2018, offering detailed analysis and additional metrics on generator performance and financial data. The digest was well-received, with positive feedback highlighting its comprehensive detail on the National Electricity Market's (NEM) generators. It was particularly praised for its insights into solar generation challenges and revenue profiles of power stations, marking it as a critical resource for understanding market dynamics. Compliments from various media and clients emphasized its value as a detailed and accessible source of information. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Demand for comprehensive market analysis and insights. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-01-2020, 29-01-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar energy investors, energy market analysts, financial institutions. Its cause type is: Market information dissemination. Its causes are: Updating the Generator Report Card, providing insights into solar generation and market dynamics, revealing revenue profiles of power stations.

title: UQ presents webinar on the Q1 performance of their 1.1MW Tesla battery
author: Andrew Wilson
classified_content: The summary of the news "UQ presents webinar on the Q1 performance of their 1.1MW Tesla battery" posted at 08-06-2020 10:46:00 AM is: The University of Queensland (UQ) installed a 1.1MW/2.15MWh Tesla Powerpack system, Queensland’s largest behind-the-meter battery, in late 2019. The project team published a report on its Q1 2020 performance in May 2020, discussing the battery's potential to provide 'virtual cap contracts.' A webinar was held in late May to discuss the report, which generated substantial interest and an extensive Q&A based on unanswered questions. UQ aims to annually report and share insights on the battery's performance to promote knowledge sharing. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Energy transition discussions and performance evaluation of a Tesla battery installation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-06-2020, late May 2020, May 2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Initiatives and presentations. Its causes are: The need for evaluation of energy storage systems, performance reporting, interest in renewable energy initiatives.

title: Two units at Vales Point trip simultaneously on Sunday 7th June 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two units at Vales Point trip simultaneously on Sunday 7th June 2020" posted at 07-06-2020 03:05:00 PM is: On Saturday, June 6th, Vales Point Power Station experienced two separate instances of non-conformance involving unit 5. However, a more significant incident occurred on Sunday, June 7th, when both units 5 and 6 simultaneously tripped at 13:33, leading to a non-credible contingency event. AEMO reported the incident but did not indicate any load shedding or disconnection of bulk electrical load. The cause of this event remains unknown. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-06-2020. Its affected region is: NSW1. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Simultaneous tripping of Vales Point units 5 and 6.

title: Not as simple as it appears – estimating curtailment of renewable generation
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Not as simple as it appears – estimating curtailment of renewable generation" posted at 05-06-2020 09:37:00 PM is: The article discusses the complexities involved in estimating and attributing the curtailment of wind and solar energy production in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The Australian Energy Market Operator’s (AEMO) Quarterly Energy Dynamics report provides curtailment estimates, but the process is intricate due to assumptions and the lack of precise data. Key curtailment causes include network constraints, economic factors, and commissioning processes. Valuing curtailed output at a single generator level is straightforward, but understanding its market-wide implications, particularly at a regional level, is more complicated due to factors like negative pricing and non-scheduled generation curtailment. Finally, attributing curtailment causes is challenging due to the complex nature of the linear-program dispatch approach used by AEMO and the variability introduced by multiple simultaneous constraints, bidding strategies, and real-time changes.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, as curtailment occurs routinely and varies with market conditions. Its root_cause is: Network constraints, economic factors, and generator commissioning. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-06-2020. Its affected region is: Unknown, but implicitly the National Electricity Market in Australia. Its affected key users are: Renewable energy project owners and developers. Its cause type is: Market and operational constraints. Its causes are: Network constraints, negative spot prices, generator commissioning processes.

title: AER to develop two Rule Change proposals about Semi-Scheduled generation
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER to develop two Rule Change proposals about Semi-Scheduled generation" posted at 31-05-2020 04:27:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has been requested by the COAG Energy Council to develop two rule change proposals to address operational challenges faced by Semi-Scheduled generators in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The first proposal mandates continuous disclosure of any capacity restrictions due to physical factors to the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), responding to past instances where AEMO felt inadequately informed. The second proposal requires semi-scheduled generators to adhere to their dispatch targets akin to scheduled generators, raising questions about the scalability and sustainability of current operations as the market transitions. These proposals align with broader considerations of establishing a Two-Sided Market, raising concerns about potential overlap with other regulatory reviews. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Challenges with operational transparency and adherence to dispatch targets of Semi-Scheduled generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-05-2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Operators of Semi-Scheduled generators and AEMO. Its cause type is: Regulatory and operational. Its causes are: Insufficient reporting on capacity restrictions and non-adherence to dispatch targets by Semi-Scheduled generators.

title: Small Solar Should Embrace Control
author: Tom Geiser
classified_content: The summary of the news "Small Solar Should Embrace Control" posted at 25-05-2020 01:25:00 PM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) plan to curtail small rooftop solar installations under certain conditions, which sparked strong negative reactions from residential solar owners. The author argues that centralised control of solar production is necessary for grid stability and offers financial incentives for solar owners. A coordinated system is needed to prevent large fluctuations in solar generation, which could threaten grid stability. The article promotes responsive, proportional control measures instead of binary on-off controls, suggesting gradual production adjustments based on price changes to provide predictable behavior for grid demand forecasting. The author calls on the solar industry and state regulators to support more cost-reflective tariffs and improved inverter controllability to manage solar production better. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as changes in solar control mechanisms and policies would affect future market stability. Its root_cause is: The need for grid stability and economic incentives for solar power. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-05-2020. Its affected region is: Australia, specifically South Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential solar owners. Its cause type is: Market reform and policy change. Its causes are: AEMO's initiative to curtail rooftop solar, the need for centralized control to maintain grid stability, and economic incentives for solar owners to manage their production proactively.

title: Sunshine State forgets its own branding on Saturday 23rd May 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sunshine State forgets its own branding on Saturday 23rd May 2020" posted at 24-05-2020 09:58:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of adverse weather in Queensland on May 23, 2020, leading to significant declines in solar power production due to cloud cover. This situation highlighted the NEM's growing dependency on weather, impacting both large-scale and rooftop solar output significantly. Consequently, electricity consumption grew markedly due to increased heating needs and reduced solar power, leading to a notable rise in grid demand, primarily supplied by coal and gas generation. The event served as a critical reminder of the importance of designing adequate storage and dispatchable plant capacities for contingency in the energy transition process; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Weather-induced decrease in solar output. Its accident scale is: Region-wide (Queensland). Its dates are: 23-05-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Queensland electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Natural cause (weather). Its causes are: Cloud cover reducing solar power generation, increased electricity demand due to colder temperatures leading to a higher reliance on coal and gas power.

title: Villain no 6d – Diversity is not a magic wand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Villain no 6d – Diversity is not a magic wand" posted at 24-05-2020 09:45:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges faced in the Queensland region due to reliance on renewable energy sources like solar and wind. On 23rd May, widespread cloud cover significantly reduced the output from solar farms and rooftop PV systems, highlighting the limitations of diversity in renewable energy sources. It argues that while diversity in energy sources is important, it is not a catch-all solution for electricity supply challenges, and further analysis is needed to understand the frequency and duration of low-output periods. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Cloud cover reducing solar energy output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-05-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Solar energy users and utilities. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Widespread cloud cover leading to decreased solar production.

title: ‘Maximising Value in the NEM’, with a particular focus on Large Solar Farms
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Maximising Value in the NEM’, with a particular focus on Large Solar Farms" posted at 21-05-2020 05:00:00 PM is: The article discusses a webinar presented by Marcelle Gannon and Jonathon Dyson on May 21, 2020, focusing on maximizing value in the National Electricity Market (NEM) with specific attention to large solar farms. Conducted as part of the Clean Energy Council's series, the event replaced the annual 'Solar Industry Forum' due to COVID-19 restrictions. It featured analysis on spot price trends in Queensland and strategies to enhance income from semi-scheduled plants such as large solar facilities. The presentation, lasting 90 minutes and involving 100 attendees, highlighted the complexities involved in optimizing financial outcomes from solar energy generation.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. The root_cause is: COVID-19 restrictions leading to the adaptation of the annual event to a virtual format. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 21-05-2020. The affected region is: Queensland (discussion focus). The affected key users are: Unknown. The cause type is: Event adaptation due to external circumstances. The causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: The inability to hold the annual 'Solar Industry Forum' in Brisbane due to COVID-19 isolation requirements.

title: Recent trends in spot price distribution in QLD
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Recent trends in spot price distribution in QLD" posted at 18-05-2020 03:42:00 PM is: The article discusses the shifting patterns in the Queensland spot market, with a focus on the times of day when electricity prices are at their lowest. It highlights changes from 2017 through 2019, noting a trend where the middle of the day sees lower prices due to increased solar generation. Questions are raised about the implications for solar farm operators in 2020 with ongoing increases in generation capacity and the importance of timing for maximizing value in the NEM.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term. Its root_cause is: Increasing rooftop PV and large-scale solar capacity affecting demand and supply dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 18-05-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users is: Solar farm operators. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes is: Increasing rooftop PV installations, addition of large-scale solar capacity, changing distribution of generation throughout the day.

title: Reasonably widespread deviations delivers highest ‘aggregate Raw Off-Target’ for the 2019 year for ‘all Wind’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reasonably widespread deviations delivers highest ‘aggregate Raw Off-Target’ for the 2019 year for ‘all Wind’" posted at 13-05-2020 11:48:00 AM is: The news article discusses extreme deviations in the performance of wind-related energy dispatch in 2019, highlighting a significant aggregate 'Raw Off-Target' for wind units on September 1, 2019, with a deviation of +375MW from the expected dispatch target. This discrepancy primarily affected the South Australian and Victoria regions due to unexpected changes in wind patterns, with the Macarthur Wind Farm and Snowtown Group contributing significantly to the deviation. The analysis underscores the need for better forecasting and systemic planning in the evolving NEM 2.0 market to mitigate risks of grid/system instability in the future.; Its relevance level is: Level 1: Most Relevant. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and potentially influences future market stabilization efforts. Its root cause is: Unexpected weather changes leading to underperformance of wind farms. Its accident scale is: No physical damage reported but significant operational impact. Its dates are: 01-09-2019. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Grid operators and energy consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Weather-induced operational risk. Its causes are: Sudden drop in wind speeds, inaccuracies in Australian Wind Energy Forecasting System (AWEFS), synchronization issues within the semi-scheduled dispatch framework.

title: Can batteries replace ‘cap’ contracts to hedge customer load? A case study from Q1 2020
author: Andrew Wilson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Can batteries replace ‘cap’ contracts to hedge customer load? A case study from Q1 2020" posted at 11-05-2020 02:12:00 PM is: The University of Queensland (UQ) explored the potential of using its 1.1MW behind-the-meter Tesla Powerpack battery as a virtual cap contract to hedge against electricity market price spikes in Q1 2020. The virtual cap contract aims to manage spot price risk by using stored energy during high price intervals exceeding $300/MWh, partially replacing traditional financial cap contracts. Although the virtual cap provided a volume coverage of 58% and a financial coverage of 53%, generating a net value of $19,415 compared to the financial cap alternative, it faced challenges due to limited storage duration and some forecasting errors. These findings highlight the potential benefits and limitations of battery systems in energy market risk management, while also emphasizing the need for further optimization of control algorithms. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Q1 2020. Its root cause is: Exploration of battery utilization for market price hedging. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: January to March 2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: The University of Queensland. Its cause type is: Strategic initiative. Its causes are: Market price volatility, battery’s performance limitations, forecasting challenges.

title: Case Study about Non-Conformance with Yarranlea Solar Farm on Friday 1st May 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study about Non-Conformance with Yarranlea Solar Farm on Friday 1st May 2020" posted at 11-05-2020 09:20:00 AM is: The article discusses a case of non-conformance involving the Yarranlea Solar Farm, which occurred on Friday, 1st May 2020 in the NEM. The day was marked by low demand, high solar generation, and transmission constraints in northern NSW, leading to negative prices in the Queensland region. As a result, some solar farms, including Yarranlea, curtailed generation. AEMO flagged Yarranlea Solar Farm as non-conforming due to failure to adhere to dispatch targets for a consecutive 95-minute period. This non-conformance was highlighted as part of broader market dynamics, including grid conditions and operational challenges faced by solar farms in the Queensland region. Yarranlea's operational issues were partly attributed to inadequate rebidding practices and the solar farm's remote location. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (a few days). Its root_cause is: Non-conformance due to market conditions and operational challenges. Its accident scale is: N/A (not an accident). Its dates is: 01-05-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD1). Its affected key users is: Solar farm operators in Queensland. Its cause type is: Market and operational factors. Its causes is: Low demand, high solar generation, transmission constraints, and inadequate rebidding practices.

title: Stanwell unit operation on 12th October 2019 delivers most negative ‘aggregate Raw Off-Target’ for the 2019 year for ‘all Coal’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Stanwell unit operation on 12th October 2019 delivers most negative ‘aggregate Raw Off-Target’ for the 2019 year for ‘all Coal’" posted at 06-05-2020 09:20:00 AM is: This article explores the significant negative aggregate Raw Off-Target occurrence for all coal units at 15:05 on 12th October 2019, highlighting the over-performance of Stanwell Unit 1, which resulted in a -357MW deviation. The article is part of a set examining the performance of different energy unit groupings in 2019, focusing on deviations from dispatch targets. Stanwell Unit 1 was involved in a 'trip to house load' test, leading to an unexpected over-performance period. Other coal units had minimal contributions or were on target; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term due to immediate grid balancing during the test period but may have insights for future grid management. Its root_cause is: Testing regime change at Stanwell Unit 1. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-10-2019. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (specifically areas relying on Stanwell's output). Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators and coal unit managers. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Stanwell Unit 1 testing which led to over-performance during attempts to ramp down and power was maintained for longer than anticipated.

title: Kogan Creek trip on 9th October 2019 delivers highest ‘aggregate Raw Off-Target’ for the 2019 year for ‘all Coal’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Kogan Creek trip on 9th October 2019 delivers highest ‘aggregate Raw Off-Target’ for the 2019 year for ‘all Coal’" posted at 05-05-2020 04:40:00 PM is: The article discusses an analysis of an unexpected trip at Kogan Creek power station on October 9, 2019, highlighting its impact on the electricity market. This event led to the highest aggregate Raw Off-Target deviation for coal-fired units in 2019, at +709MW, representing a 5.0% deviation from the dispatch target. The Kogan Creek incident was due to technical issues requiring AVR repairs, and was not completely unexpected as the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had been alerted to problems shortly before the trip. The event underscores challenges in balancing grid supply and demand and serves as a case study in electricity market contingencies.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, occurring on the day of the incident (09-10-2019). Its root_cause is: Technical failure at Kogan Creek power station. Its accident scale is: Significant, affecting aggregate coal-fired performance. Its dates is: 09-10-2019. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity providers and market operators. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes is: Kogan Creek's technical issues leading to a trip, unexpected unit responses at other stations.

title: Balancing Supply and Demand …. in the fast approaching ‘NEM 2.0’ world
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Balancing Supply and Demand …. in the fast approaching ‘NEM 2.0’ world" posted at 05-05-2020 01:02:00 PM is: The article discusses the impending transition to a 'NEM 2.0,' addressing challenges of integrating renewable energy into the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the need to manage supply-demand balance amidst this transition, examining the performance of Semi-Scheduled and Scheduled plants and their implications. The article points to various considerations with the current system, particularly concerning the emerging two-sided market and 'Raw Off-Target' issues, indicating a necessity for market design that ensures stability and sustainability as the reliance on renewable energy increases.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The preparation for adapting the NEM to incorporate higher renewable energy integration. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-05-2020 01:02:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Market participants, large energy users, indirect stakeholders. Its cause type is: Market reform and policy development. Its causes are: Challenges with integration of intermittent renewable energy in NEM, need for design of NEM 2.0, and 'Raw Off-Target' performance issues.

title: Quarterly Renewables Wrap – Q1 2020
author: Ben Willacy
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quarterly Renewables Wrap – Q1 2020" posted at 05-05-2020 12:13:00 PM is: The article discusses the growth and challenges faced by the renewable energy sector within the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the first quarter of 2020. The NEM saw significant growth in wind and solar capacity, with 436 MW becoming fully operational and 831 MW across six projects connecting to the grid. Notably, new project commitments surged to nearly 1 GW, largely driven by the Queensland Renewables 400 auction. However, grid connection delays, particularly in Victoria and southern New South Wales, have postponed full integration of around 50% of the mechanically completed projects, and although inverter issues in some solar farms have been resolved, future integrations face uncertainty. The article anticipates a strong second quarter influenced by several factors, including the construction completion of Stockyard Hill wind farm in Victoria. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions, grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to near-term with potential delays. Its root_cause is: Grid connection issues and project delays. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-05-2020 (same date as the news date). Its affected region is: Victoria and southern New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Renewable energy project developers and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Structural/technical hurdles within the grid system. Its causes are: Grid connection problems, inverter issues, and construction delays.

title: Aggregate wind production peaked above 5,000MW for the first time (NEM-wide) – on Friday 1st May 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aggregate wind production peaked above 5,000MW for the first time (NEM-wide) – on Friday 1st May 2020" posted at 04-05-2020 02:23:00 PM is: On Friday, May 1st, 2020, aggregate wind production across all wind farms in the National Electricity Market (NEM) exceeded 5,000MW for the first time. This peak was recorded during the 22:25 dispatch interval. Despite the average capacity for May being inaccurately low due to limited days accounted for, this milestone marks significant growth since wind output surpassed 4,000MW just over a year ago. The data aligns with trends observed in wind output in the NEM, although questions about declining peak capacity factors in recent years remain. Constraints on exports from Queensland had minimal impact on this peak, given only two wind farms in the region are incorporated into the data. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, primarily affecting the date of peak output. Its root_cause is: Increase in wind farm output capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-05-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market-wide, with specific mention of Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and market. Its causes are: Wind farms reaching a new peak output capacity, minor contributions from Queensland wind farms, ongoing decline in peak capacity factor observed annually in the mid-year.

title: Spot prices plunge in QLD, and may well remain underwater for the coming week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot prices plunge in QLD, and may well remain underwater for the coming week" posted at 01-05-2020 03:29:00 PM is: The article discusses the plunge of spot prices in Queensland on May 1, 2020, due to a combination of factors including high production from rooftop PV systems, reduced demand for space cooling owing to a cold front, and transmission outages affecting export capabilities. The limited export capacity over the QNI interconnector, caused by a transmission outage in northern NSW, exacerbated the negative pricing outcomes. Furthermore, significant production from large solar plants and changes in generation bids to avoid negative pricing were observed. Non-conformance issues at the Yarranlea and Clermont Solar Farms also contributed to the situation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: For the coming week. Its root cause is: High rooftop PV production, cold front reducing demand, and a transmission outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 01-05-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Multiple factors including grid instability and market dynamics. Its causes is: High solar production, reduced cooling demand, transmission outage, non-conformance at solar farms, export constraint over QNI.

title: AEMO releases its first* ‘Renewable Integration Study’ on 30th April 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases its first* ‘Renewable Integration Study’ on 30th April 2020" posted at 30-04-2020 12:06:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published its first 'Renewable Integration Study,' which examines the evolving challenges and potential solutions for integrating renewable energy sources into the National Electricity Market (NEM). The release highlights the need for reforms to ensure grid stability and manage the increasing volatility associated with higher shares of renewable energy generation. The study includes detailed analyses and supplementary reports on distributed solar PV, frequency control, and system variability, emphasizing the importance of adaptive regulatory measures. Concerns over the capability of Australia's grid to handle substantial renewable contributions are addressed, proposing careful generation regulation to forestall curbs on renewable energy. The report's findings and proposed reforms have garnered media attention, underscoring the complexity and urgency of achieving a stable energy transition.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Integration of high levels of renewable energy into the grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-04-2020. Its affected region is: Primarily focused on South Australia but has NEM-wide implications. Its affected key users are: Energy policy makers, regulators, and renewable energy stakeholders. Its cause type is: Strategic study and analysis. Its causes are: Integration challenges of renewable energy, grid stability concerns, regulatory reform needs.

title: Two papers released by COAG Energy Council are early stages in fleshing out ‘NEM 2.0’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two papers released by COAG Energy Council are early stages in fleshing out ‘NEM 2.0’" posted at 25-04-2020 02:02:00 PM is: The COAG Energy Council released two discussion papers that mark the initial steps toward implementing 'NEM 2.0', a significantly reformed National Electricity Market (NEM). These papers, published on April 20, 2020, seek to elaborate on innovative market designs such as the 'two-sided market' and revised system services and ahead markets as part of an energy transition. Feedback is requested by May 18, 2020, with public consultations planned for later this year; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Policy development for energy transition in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-04-2020 and 20-04-2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Policy-based. Its causes are: Development of a 'two-sided market', changes in system services, and ahead markets proposals.

title: Observing Easter Saturday, a little belatedly
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Observing Easter Saturday, a little belatedly" posted at 19-04-2020 04:29:00 PM is: On Easter Saturday, April 11, 2020, renewable energy sources such as wind and solar reached a milestone by supplying over 50% of the National Electricity Market (NEM) consumption during several half-hour periods. South Australia even achieved more than 100% of local demand from renewables with excess exported to other regions. However, the NEM faced challenges that day; in Victoria, three units at Yallourn coal plant tripped likely due to lightning, temporarily reducing coal output and increasing reliance on renewables. Meanwhile, South Australian wind output experienced sharp fluctuations due to system constraints aimed at maintaining grid strength, with semi-scheduled generators offering prices at the market floor in reaction to negative market conditions. Despite these events, the day's overall plentiful renewable supply mitigated significant price impacts. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term, mainly on April 11, 2020. Its root_cause is: Integration of high levels of renewable energy leading to system constraints and weather-related disruptions (lightning). Its accident scale is: Large, impacting the state's grid operations and NEM price fluctuations. Its dates are: 11-04-2020. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, and broader NEM regions. Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators and significant energy market participants. Its cause type is: Natural phenomena and system operation constraints. Its causes are: High renewable penetration, lightning strikes on transmission, system strength constraints, fluctuations in market offers due to negative pricing.

title: Careful with the ‘doomsday claims’ that COVID responses have (or will) massively reduced aggregate underlying electricity consumption in the NEM….
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Impact of Coronavirus on the NEM from March 2020
classified_content: The summary of the news "Careful with the ‘doomsday claims’ that COVID responses have (or will) massively reduced aggregate underlying electricity consumption in the NEM…." posted at 16-04-2020 06:53:00 PM is: The article challenges the prevalent media narrative that COVID-19 responses have significantly reduced electricity consumption in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). Consulting firm Reputex's modeling forecasts anticipated a temporary reduction in electricity demand due to COVID-19, with potential impacts on electricity prices and coal generation viability. The commentary, however, argues that such claims are exaggerated and not supported by current NEM data, which shows no substantial reduction in overall electricity consumption. The author urges careful interpretation of data to avoid misleading conclusions about electricity demand trends during the pandemic.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (April and May 2020). Its root_cause is: COVID-19 responses and societal changes due to the pandemic. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-04-2020. Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and industry stakeholders. Its cause type is: Perceived demand reduction due to COVID-19 impacts. Its causes are: Anticipated reduction in electricity demand, media reports of consumption decline, predictions of impacts on coal generators.

title: Four units trip in Victoria on Easter Saturday
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four units trip in Victoria on Easter Saturday" posted at 11-04-2020 04:26:00 PM is: On Easter Saturday, four coal-generating units at the Yallourn Power Station in Victoria, Australia, tripped, alongside a significant reduction in supply from the Macarthur Wind Farm. This incident was potentially linked to a lightning strike affecting the transmission network. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued market notices reclassifying these events as credible contingencies due to lightning. There were no load shedding instructions or significant disconnections of bulk electrical load as a consequence of the incident. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: April 11, 2020, until further notice based on AEMO's reclassification. Its root_cause is: Suspected lightning strike affecting transmission networks. Its accident scale is: Localized within the Vic1 region. Its dates are: 11-04-2020. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (specifically the area connected via Hazelwood – Rowville lines). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Transmission network disruption. Its causes are: Lightning strike impacting Hazelwood – Rowville 220kV lines and tripping of Yallourn generating units.

title: Do you know the difference between Virtual Inertia and Fast Frequency Response?
author: Stephen Sproul
classified_content: The summary of the news "Do you know the difference between Virtual Inertia and Fast Frequency Response?" posted at 07-04-2020 11:51:00 AM is: The article explains the distinction between virtual inertia and fast frequency response (FFR) in the context of power grid stability, particularly referencing the Dalrymple Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in South Australia. Virtual inertia, provided by grid forming inverters like Dalrymple BESS, delivers an immediate response to grid frequency changes without measurement delays, unlike FFR, which relies on grid measurement and experiences inherent delays. This difference is key as power systems increasingly incorporate renewable energy and face the retirement of synchronous generators. The article emphasizes the importance of understanding these distinct mechanisms to maintain grid stability and efficiency in the evolving National Electricity Market (NEM); Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: South Australia separation from NEM causing grid frequency instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16/11/2019. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical/grid event. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: The separation of South Australia’s power grid from NEM led to frequency variations requiring intervention by systems such as the Dalrymple BESS using virtual inertia.

title: Semi-scheduled Generation Availability Forecasts–how to improve?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Semi-scheduled Generation Availability Forecasts–how to improve?" posted at 03-04-2020 09:59:00 AM is: The article explores the challenges and suggestions for improving forecasts of semi-scheduled generation availability, particularly during extreme weather conditions like high temperatures, which have an impact on intermittent generators such as wind and solar. A clear understanding and improved communication between the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and generation participants are necessary, especially regarding the roles and data used in forecasting availability. Key recommendations include enhancing the usability of the AEMO EMMS portal, providing clearer guidelines on forecasting responsibilities, and improving the capability of AWEFS and ASEFS forecasting systems. Improvements can lead to better power system security and more accurate reserve calculations.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and potentially impactful during future high-temperature events. Its root_cause is: The need for improved accuracy and understanding in forecasting systems for intermittent generation availability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-04-2020. Its affected region is: Unknown (implied to be areas under AEMO’s jurisdiction). Its affected key users are: Intermittent generation operators (wind and solar) and AEMO. Its cause type is: Procedural and systemic inefficiencies. Its causes are: Challenges in accurately forecasting generator availability during high-temperature conditions, unclear guidelines from AEMO, and technological limitations in existing forecasting tools.

title: Electricity demand as a Performance Metric, in the age of coronavirus
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Impact of Coronavirus on the NEM from March 2020
classified_content: The summary of the news "Electricity demand as a Performance Metric, in the age of coronavirus" posted at 31-03-2020 10:20:00 PM is: The article discusses the complex impact of COVID-19 on Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), focusing on the difficulties of using electricity demand as a reliable performance metric. It highlights that changes in electricity demand can be misleading indicators of both economic activity and the effectiveness of coronavirus measures due to various influencing factors such as seasonal variations, weather, and solar power injections. The author stresses the need for more actionable and clear metrics to assess the impact of the coronavirus effectively, both in public health and economic terms, to inform better decision-making. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The COVID-19 pandemic and resulting lockdown measures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-03-2020 to 31-03-2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: External (Pandemic). Its causes are: COVID-19 pandemic, government response measures, changes in electricity consumption patterns.

title: ICYMI – COAG EC flags reliability framework changes
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "ICYMI – COAG EC flags reliability framework changes" posted at 25-03-2020 04:06:00 PM is: The COAG Energy Council agreed on significant changes to the National Electricity Market's reliability arrangements, including alterations to the Retailer Reliability Obligation (RRO) and the creation of an "out of market capacity reserve." The RRO's EUSE threshold will be lowered, making it easier to trigger, and the T-1 stage can be initiated independently of the T-3 stage. The new "out of market capacity reserve" extends the RERT mechanism, allowing procurement on a three-year basis with a stricter reliability threshold. The changes aim to improve reliability but details are still pending. These interim steps will be part of an overall review of the RRO by July 2023; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: COAG Energy Council policy decision. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-03-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Retailers and certain end users. Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes are: Changes in RRO thresholds, de-linking T-1 and T-3 stages, creation of out-of-market capacity reserve.

title: 1st thoughts on possible impacts of COVID-19 on Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "1st thoughts on possible impacts of COVID-19 on Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM)" posted at 24-03-2020 07:17:00 PM is: The article analyzes the potential impacts of COVID-19 on Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), emphasizing the risks to the core electricity supply chain, potential changes in electricity demand, and broader impacts on the energy sector. Key concerns include operational disruptions due to key person risks, a potential reduction in electricity demand due to economic slowdown, and delays in new generation projects.
